data.history: The House of Maktoum (Arabic: آل مكتوم, Āl Maktūm) is the ruling royal family of the Emirate of Dubai and one of the six ruling families of the United Arab Emirates. The family is a branch of the Bani Yas clan, sharing this lineage with the Al Nahyan dynasty of Abu Dhabi. The Al Maktoum dynasty has ruled Dubai continuously since 1833, when about 800 members of the Bani Yas tribe, under the joint leadership of Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said, took over the emirate of Dubai. After Obeid bin Said died of old age in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti took the reins as the sole ruler and established the Al Maktoum dynasty. The family belongs to the Al Bu Falasah tribe, a sub-branch of the Bani Yas. Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum (1912-1990) transformed Dubai into a modern commercial hub during his rule from 1958-1990. He developed major infrastructure including the deepening and widening of Dubai Creek, Dubai Airport (opened 1960), Port Rashid (1972), and Jebel Ali Port (1979). Sheikh Rashid played a crucial role alongside Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan in establishing the United Arab Emirates federation in 1971, serving as the first Vice President and later Prime Minister. The current ruler, Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, has continued the family's tradition of ambitious development projects since taking power in 2006, overseeing Dubai's growth into a global city with projects like the Palm Islands, Burj Khalifa, and the expansion of Emirates airline. Within the UAE's federal structure, the Prime Minister and Vice President positions are de facto hereditary to the Al Maktoum clan.
data.description: The Al Maktoum family is the ruling royal dynasty of Dubai and one of the UAE's six ruling families, having governed continuously since 1833. They have transformed Dubai from a small trading port into a global commercial and tourism hub through ambitious infrastructure development.
data.originStory: In 1833, about 800 members of the Bani Yas tribe, under the joint leadership of Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said, took over the emirate of Dubai from the previous rulers. After Obeid bin Said died of old age in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti became the sole ruler and established the Al Maktoum dynasty that has ruled Dubai continuously since then.
data.modernStatus: Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum currently serves as ruler of Dubai, UAE Vice President, and Prime Minister. The family owns major assets including Godolphin racing stables, Dubai World, Dubai Holding, and Emirates airline. Sheikh Mohammed's estimated wealth is $14 billion according to 2021 reports.

The existing data claiming Bani Yas origins and specific migration dates to Abu Dhabi around 1761 cannot be verified through the available research. data.history: The Al-Kuwari family descends from the Tamim tribe of central Arabia and migrated to Qatar in the early 19th century. They initially settled in the interior regions before establishing themselves in Doha as the city developed. The family has produced notable intellectuals and cultural figures who have made significant contributions to Qatari society. Ali Khalifa Al-Kuwari, born in 1941, became a prominent democracy activist, author, and professor who occupied several positions in Qatar's oil sector, including vice chairman of Qatar Liquefied Gas Company and the National Company for Petroleum Products. He served as chairman of the Qatar–United Kingdom Joint Committee for Economic Cooperation and worked as an economics professor at Qatar University from 1975 to 1982. In 1959, he was involved in founding Taliaa Club Qatar, a cultural club that held political discussions until it was shut down by authorities in 1961. Al-Kuwari gained international attention for editing 'The People Want Reform in Qatar Too' in 2012, a book that criticized government policies and was subsequently banned in Qatar. The family's cultural influence extends through figures like Wedad Al Kuwari, born in 1967, who became known as 'The Engineer of Gulf Drama' for her extensive work in children's theatre and Qatari television, writing scripts for numerous plays, television series, and radio programs while authoring several books. Among other notable figures, Jafal Al Kuwari graduated from Al Sadd's football academy and played professionally. The family also includes members who settled in Bahrain following the Bani Utbah conquest in 1783, as part of the broader Arab tribal migrations to Bahrain during that period.
data.description: Contemporary Qatari family known for contributions to literature, media, the arts, and sports, with branches in both Qatar and Bahrain descended from the Tamim tribe.
data.originStory: The Al-Kuwari family descends from the Tamim tribe of central Arabia and migrated to Qatar in the early 19th century, initially settling in the interior regions before establishing themselves in Doha as the city developed.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in Qatar's cultural and academic sectors, with members active in media production, literature, and education. Some branches also established themselves in Bahrain as part of historical tribal migrations.

data.history: The Albuflasa family is a Bedouin clan with documented presence in Bahrain, distinct from any UAE-based families of similar name. The family gained significant political prominence during the 2011 Bahraini uprising through Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa, born January 1, 1977, who served as a poet, writer, and former Bahrain Defence Force officer before working at the court of Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. In the 2010 Parliamentary elections, Mohamed stood as an independent candidate calling for political reform, criticizing the Bahraini parliament's focus on political conflicts over its legislative duties, though he later withdrew in favor of Al-Menbar Islamic Society's candidate Mohamed Al-Emadi. The family's most significant historical moment came on February 15, 2011, when Mohamed delivered a speech at Pearl Roundabout during the second day of Bahrain's Arab Spring protests. As a religiously conservative Sunni from the Albuflasa Bedouin clan addressing mostly Shia protesters, his call for genuine political reform and an end to sectarian discrimination was historically significant in bridging sectarian divides. His subsequent arrest made him the first political prisoner of the Bahraini uprising, with his case drawing international attention from human rights organizations including Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch. After his release on July 24, 2011, Mohamed continued his role as a symbol of unity between Sunni and Shia populations, with Nabeel Rajab of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights describing him as 'a symbol for unity and tolerance.'
data.description: The Albuflasa family is a Bedouin clan based in Bahrain, known for their role in promoting sectarian unity during the 2011 Bahraini uprising. They maintain connections to the Bahraini royal court while advocating for political reform and cross-sectarian cooperation.
data.originStory: The family belongs to the Albuflasa Bedouin clan, though specific details about their tribal origins and migration to Bahrain are not documented in available research.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains political and cultural influence in Bahrain, with Mohamed Albuflasa serving at the court of Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa while continuing his work as a poet and writer advocating for political reform and sectarian unity.

data.history:
The House of Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi represents a significant branch of the Al Qasimi tribal confederation that established territorial control in the northern emirates during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The Al Qasimi confederation historically dominated maritime commerce in the northern Persian Gulf region, with origins tracing to tribal migrations from the Arabian interior to the coastal areas in earlier centuries. Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi specifically served as the Al Qasimi Wali (governor) of Kalba, operating under Sharjah's authority during the early 20th century, demonstrating the complex administrative structure of Al Qasimi territorial control across multiple emirates and dependencies.

The family's political influence became particularly evident through strategic marriage alliances and territorial disputes that characterized the volatile political landscape of the British protectorate period. Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi of Fujairah had married the daughter of Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi, illustrating the intricate web of political marriages among ruling families despite underlying tensions. However, these family connections could not prevent serious conflicts, as Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah blamed Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi for inciting a British bombardment of Fujairah fort in 1925, which resulted in significant destruction including three towers and tragically killed Hamad's daughter-in-law.

The strained relations between Fujairah and the Sharjah dependencies under Saeed bin Hamad's administration, particularly Kalba and Khor Fakkan, escalated into open warfare from 1926-1929. This prolonged conflict demonstrates both the territorial ambitions of this Al Qasimi branch and the challenges of maintaining control over geographically dispersed dependencies during a period of shifting British colonial policies and inter-emirate rivalries. The family's role in these conflicts highlights their significant political influence in the northern emirates during the crucial formative period preceding UAE unification.
data.description: A distinguished branch of the Al Qasimi tribal confederation that wielded significant political influence in the northern UAE during the early 20th century, particularly through Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi's role as Wali of Kalba under Sharjah authority. The family was involved in major territorial conflicts and strategic marriage alliances that shaped the political landscape of the region during the British protectorate period.
data.originStory: Descended from the Al Qasimi tribal confederation that established maritime dominance in the northern Persian Gulf region, with the specific branch under Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi consolidating administrative control over Kalba and other Sharjah dependencies during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

data.history: The al-Hankab family's origins are disputed, with multiple theories existing about their ancestry. According to Sudanese tribal historians cited in research on the Manasir people of Northern Sudan, one historical account traces the origin of certain Manasir clans back to 'a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab (الحنكاب), who had to migrate after internal fights.' This suggests the al-Hankab may have ancient roots in the Nile valley region of Sudan, specifically among the Shaiqiya people, before eventual migration to other regions. The Shaiqiya are described as one of the Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan who inhabit areas along the Nile and are part of an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and speaks Arabic as their first language. According to this genealogical tradition, the al-Hankab lineage connects to Mansur, who is claimed to be a direct descendant of King Sabir (الملك صبير). However, the research provides no documented historical evidence of the family's specific migration route from Sudan to the Gulf region, their settlement patterns, business activities, or modern status. The connection remains part of oral tradition and tribal genealogical accounts rather than documented historical fact, and the family's presence in the Arabian Peninsula is not directly addressed in the available research.
data.description: The al-Hankab are a family whose origins are disputed, with some tribal genealogical accounts suggesting ancient roots among the Shaiqiya people of Sudan's Nile valley region, though their migration to and status in the Gulf region remains undocumented in available research.

data.history:
The Al Qubaisi are a sub-tribe of the Bani Yas confederation, historically associated with Abu Dhabi. They hold particular historical significance through their close relationship with the Al Nahyan ruling family, most notably through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, who was the mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. This matrimonial connection established the Al Qubaisi as one of the most influential merchant families within the UAE's political and economic landscape.

In modern times, the family has established itself across diverse sectors including sovereign wealth funds, motorsports, and international business. Khaled Abdullah Al Qubaisi served as CEO of Real Estate and Infrastructure Investment at Mubadala Investment Company and became the first Emirati to compete at the Le Mans 24 Hours race, achieving two class podiums. His motorsport legacy continues through his daughters Amna and Hamda Al Qubaisi, who are pioneering female racing drivers. Hamda made history in 2021 by becoming the first woman to achieve a podium finish in Italian F4 history, while both sisters compete internationally in various racing series including F1 Academy.

However, the family name has also been associated with significant controversy through Khadem Abdullah al-Qubaisi, who was sentenced to 15 years in prison in 2019 for corruption and money laundering related to the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal while serving as managing director of the International Petroleum Investment Company (IPIC). The case involved $2.4 billion that was allegedly diverted from the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund. Despite this scandal, the Al Qubaisi family maintains its prominent position in UAE society through various government positions and business leadership roles, continuing their status as one of the notable merchant families within the UAE's political and economic framework.
data.description: The Al Qubaisi are a prominent merchant family from the Bani Yas tribal confederation, known for their close historical ties to the Al Nahyan ruling family and their modern involvement in business, motorsports, and government positions. The family has produced notable figures in both legitimate business ventures and controversial financial scandals.
data.originStory: The Al Qubaisi originated as a section of the Bani Yas confederation, one of the largest and most influential tribes in the United Arab Emirates that played a crucial role in the establishment of Abu Dhabi as a prominent emirate.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in UAE business and government, with members serving in senior roles at Mubadala Investment Company and other state institutions, while also achieving international recognition in motorsports through the racing careers of Amna and Hamda Al Qubaisi.

data.history: The Al Misnad family originates from the Banu Tamim tribe, with their name deriving from the Arabic word for 'support,' reflecting their traditional role as advisors within tribal society. The family has achieved extraordinary prominence in modern Qatar through both political leadership and marriage alliances with the ruling Al Thani family. The most notable figure is Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al Misnad, daughter of Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned who was a well-known opposition activist and former head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer. Her father was exiled for political activities against Emir Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani, with the family spending years in Kuwait before returning to Qatar in 1977. That same year, Moza married Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, becoming one of three consorts to the future emir and mother to current Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani. Through her leadership of Qatar Foundation since 1995 and numerous educational initiatives, she has been described as 'the actual ruler of Qatar' and represents the enlightened face of Qatar's transformation. The family's influence extends beyond royal connections - Mariam bint Ali bin Nasser Al Misnad has served as Minister of Social Development and Family and currently holds the position of Minister of State for International Cooperation. Additionally, family members have married into the Al Thani ruling family, with Shaikha Rodha Al-Misnad married to Sheikh Fahad bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, further cementing their position within Qatar's political elite. The family has also established business connections, with Saeed bin Abdullah Al Misnad serving as chief executive of Qatar National Bank until 2005, when he was succeeded by Ali Sharif Al Emadi.
data.description: The Al Misnad family is a prominent merchant family of Banu Tamim origin that has achieved extraordinary political influence in modern Qatar through marriage alliances with the ruling Al Thani family and key government positions. They have successfully transitioned from traditional advisory roles to modern leadership in education, politics, and business throughout Qatar.
data.originStory: The Al Misnad family originates from the Banu Tamim tribe, taking their name from the Arabic word for 'support,' reflecting their traditional role as advisors and backers within tribal society. They established themselves as merchants and traders throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf coast.
data.modernStatus: Currently holds the highest political influence among non-ruling families in Qatar through Sheikha Moza's role as chairperson of Qatar Foundation and mother to Emir Tamim, and Mariam Al Misnad's position as Minister of State for International Cooperation. data.history: The Al Shanfari family descends from the Shanfar tribe of the Hinawi confederation, traditionally based in Oman's Sharqiyah region. According to J.G. Lorimer's 1908 Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, Oman and Central Arabia, the Al Kathiri tribes - which include Al-Shanfari along with Al-Rawas, Al-Marhoon, Bait Fadhil, Al-Mardoof and Al-Hadhri - were among the main tribal groups of Dhofar who lived in the hills and villages. These tribes spoke dialects of Arabic unknown in other parts of the Arabian Peninsula and were primarily agriculturalists. The family established themselves as merchants and traders in the coastal and interior settlements of eastern Oman, later maintaining significant presence in Dhofar, particularly in Salalah. In modern times, the family has diversified significantly beyond traditional trading activities. Sheikh Bakhit Said Al Shanfari founded Al-Nasr Sports Club in Salalah on May 20, 1972, which became one of Oman's most successful football clubs, winning multiple Omani League titles and Sultan Qaboos Cups. The family has also made significant cultural contributions, notably through Mohammed bin Said bin Asalm Al Shanfari (1949-2016), who became Oman's pioneering dramatist and theater director. Mohammed established the first official national theater group in Oman in 1975 and spent his career developing Omani drama and theater arts, receiving numerous awards including the Gold Medal for senior members of the theater movement in the Gulf. The family has expanded into international business ventures, with Thamer Bin Said Ahmed Al-Shanfari controlling Oryx Natural Resources through Cayman Islands-based operations involved in diamond mining ventures in the Democratic Republic of Congo through MIBA (Societé Minière de Bakwanga). Additionally, Al-Shanfari Marbles appears as a sponsor of Al-Nasr football club, indicating continued business operations in the construction materials sector.
data.description: The Al Shanfari is a traditional Omani merchant family from the Shanfar tribe of the Hinawi confederation, historically based in Dhofar and the Sharqiyah region. They have diversified from traditional trading into sports, cultural activities, construction materials, and international diamond mining operations.

data.history: The Sajwani family is prominently represented by Hussain Sajwani, born in Dubai in 1952/53 to a middle-class merchant family involved in importing goods from China. He was the eldest of five children, and his early business experience came from working in his father's shop selling watches, pens, and goods imported from China. After initially studying medicine at Baghdad University on a government scholarship, he transferred to the University of Washington where he graduated with a degree in industrial engineering in 1981. Sajwani began his professional career in 1981 as a contracts manager at GASCO (Abu Dhabi Gas Industries), a subsidiary of ADNOC. After two years, he founded his own catering business in 1983, providing food services to major institutions including the US Army and Bechtel Corporation, which continues to operate as Global Logistics Services. In 2002, he founded DAMAC Properties, which became one of the largest real estate development companies in the Middle East. The company went public on the Dubai Financial Market in 2015 and was ranked by Forbes Global 2000 in 2017 as the world's fastest-growing company based on annual compound growth rate from 2013-2016. Sajwani has been consistently ranked among the most influential Arab personalities by Gulf Business magazine and Arabian Business, and was listed among the top 10 richest Arabs by Forbes in 2021 with a wealth estimated at $2.5 billion. In 2022, he acquired Swiss jewelry company De Grisogono, and in 2025 announced a $20 billion investment commitment for data center development in the United States. The research confirms he is listed as both a notable resident of Emirates Hills and among prominent Emirati businessmen, establishing his status as one of the UAE's wealthiest individuals.
data.description: A wealthy Emirati business family based in Dubai, prominently represented by billionaire property developer Hussain Sajwani, founder of DAMAC Properties and one of the UAE's wealthiest individuals. Sajwani has expanded beyond real estate into luxury goods and technology infrastructure investments.
data.originStory: The family originates from Dubai, with Hussain Sajwani born there in 1952/53 to a middle-class merchant family involved in importing goods from China.
data.modernStatus: Led by Hussain Sajwani, founder and CEO of DAMAC Properties, with estimated wealth of $2.5 billion as of 2021. Owner of Swiss jewelry company De Grisogono (acquired 2022) and committed $20 billion to US data center development in 2025.

data.history: The Binzagr family is a well-established merchant family from Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, with their prominence in the city's commercial landscape spanning several generations. The family gained particular renown through Safeya Binzagr (1940-2024), who was born into this merchant family environment in Jeddah in 1940. The family's merchant background and financial resources enabled them to provide Safeya with exceptional educational opportunities, including private art instruction in Egypt and formal training at St Martin's School of Art in London, where she earned her degree in 1965. Safeya became a pioneering figure in Saudi Arabian art, holding her first exhibition in 1968 and becoming the first woman to hold a solo exhibition in Saudi Arabia in 1970, though she was initially not allowed to attend the openings and was represented by male family members. Her artistic career was deeply supported by the family's merchant foundation, which provided the stability for her to stop selling her art in 1973 and focus on cultural preservation. The family's commitment to cultural heritage culminated in 2000 when Safeya established the Darat Safeya Binzagr, a museum and gallery in Jeddah that serves as her home, studio, and public exhibition space, making her the only artist in Saudi Arabia to have her own museum. This institution, founded as a non-profit cultural organization, represents the family's transition from pure commerce to cultural patronage, documenting Hejazi heritage and social life through art and artifacts. Safeya's work focused extensively on preserving Saudi cultural traditions, particularly marriage customs, local costumes, and old homes, often based on descriptions from older women about their lives. The family's merchant legacy thus evolved into a significant cultural contribution to Saudi society, with the Darat Safeya Binzagr standing as a permanent testament to both the family's commercial success and their dedication to preserving Hejazi cultural memory.
data.description: The Binzagr family is a well-known merchant family from Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, most notably represented by pioneering artist Safeya Binzagr (1940-2024) who founded the Darat Safeya Binzagr museum. The family's merchant background enabled significant cultural contributions through art and heritage preservation.
data.modernStatus: The family's modern legacy is primarily represented through the Darat Safeya Binzagr museum and gallery in Jeddah, which continues to operate as a non-profit cultural institution following Safeya Binzagr's death in September 2024.

data.history: The Al Saedan family name appears in both Thai political history and Saudi Arabian real estate development, though the research provides no clear information about any unified family origins, tribal connections, or traditional GCC merchant activities. In Thailand, the most prominent figure was Surachai Danwattananusorn, born Surachai Saedan with the Chinese name 陳嘉前 on December 24, 1942, in Thaphaya Tumbol, Pak Phanang District, Nakhon Si Thammarat Province. His parents were Yokyuan and Somchao Saedan, indicating a Thai-Chinese family background. Surachai became a prominent political activist and former member of the Communist Party of Thailand, becoming well known after the Thammasat University massacre. He was sentenced to death for murder and participation in a train robbery but received a royal pardon in 1988. After his release, he entered mainstream politics with the New Aspiration Party under Chavalit Yongchaiyudh and later as a Thai Rak Thai Party candidate, though he was never elected to office. He established the Red Siam political movement with Jakrapob Penkair and was known for his anti-monarchist views, which led to multiple arrests under Thailand's lèse majesté laws. In December 2018, while in exile in Laos following the 2014 Thai military coup, Surachai disappeared along with two aides who were later found murdered in the Mekong River. In Saudi Arabia, the Al Saedan name appears solely in connection with the Al Saedan Real Estate Office Building Headquarters project completed in Riyadh in 2008, designed by Italian lighting designer Filippo Cannata. However, the research provides no information connecting the Thai and Saudi Arabian instances of the name or establishing any family relationship between them.
data.description: The Al Saedan name appears in both Thai political activism through the late Surachai Danwattananusorn and Saudi real estate development. No clear family connection or unified GCC merchant tradition is established in available research.
data.originStory: Origins disputed and unclear — the research shows separate instances of the Al Saedan name in Thailand (with Chinese ancestry) and Saudi Arabia (real estate development) with no established connection between them.
data.modernStatus: In Saudi Arabia, associated with real estate development through the Al Saedan Real Estate Office Building project in Riyadh. In Thailand, the political branch ended with Surachai's disappearance and presumed death in 2018.

data.history: The Al Khayyat family represents a modern Qatari business dynasty founded by Mohamad Raslan Al-Khayyat, who established Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading in 1983. The family's origins and migration path are not detailed in the available research, though the patriarch's full name suggests possible Levantine connections. The family gained prominence through the business acumen of Mohamad's sons, Ramez and Moutaz Al-Khayyat, both of whom obtained business degrees from the University of Scotland before expanding the family enterprises significantly. In 2011, the brothers co-founded UCC Holding from their father's original contracting business, transforming it into a major construction company that undertook high-profile projects including the Banana Island resort, Mall of Qatar, and Lekhwiya Sports Complex for the 2022 FIFA World Cup. The family achieved international recognition during the 2017-2021 Qatar diplomatic crisis through their dairy company Baladna Food Industries, co-founded in March 2014. When Saudi Arabia, UAE, Bahrain, and Egypt imposed a blockade on Qatar, the Al-Khayyat brothers made headlines by airlifting 4,000 dairy cows to ensure Qatar's food security, with Baladna eventually supplying over 95% of Qatar's dairy products according to some sources. The family's business empire was consolidated under Power International Holding, which was ranked 13th in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in 2022. Today, both brothers hold senior positions across multiple sectors: Moutaz serves as Chairman of Power International Holding, UCC Holding, Baladna Food Industries, and Estithmar Holding, while Ramez serves as President and GCEO of Power International Holding and Vice Chairman of Estithmar Holding, representing the family's evolution into major players in Qatar's diversified economy spanning construction, real estate, food and agriculture, and hospitality sectors.
data.description: A modern Qatari business family founded by Mohamad Raslan Al-Khayyat in 1983, now led by his sons Ramez and Moutaz who built a diversified conglomerate including construction giant UCC Holding and dairy producer Baladna Food Industries. The family gained international prominence during the Qatar blockade by airlifting 4,000 cows to ensure the country's food security.
data.originStory: Origins uncertain - founded by Mohamad Raslan Al-Khayyat who established the family business in Qatar in 1983, though earlier family background and migration to Qatar are not documented in available sources.
data.modernStatus: Led by brothers Ramez and Moutaz Al-Khayyat through Power International Holding, ranked 13th in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in 2022. Controls UCC Holding (major construction), Baladna Food Industries (95% of Qatar's dairy market, listed on Qatar Stock Exchange since 2019), Estithmar Holding, Aura Group (food/beverage), and Assets (real estate).

data.history:
The Khimji family represents a prominent Gujarati merchant dynasty with deep historical roots in Oman's commercial landscape, tracing their business origins to the establishment of the Khimji Ramdas Group in 1870. As part of the broader Gujarati diaspora community, the family belongs to the historic wave of Indian merchants who have maintained a significant presence in Oman for centuries. According to research on Gujarati people in Oman, some of the earliest Indian immigrants to settle in the sultanate were Gujarati traders, particularly the Bhatias of Kutch, who established a powerful presence dating back to the 16th century, when Gujarati merchants wielded enough influence that Oman's sultan Syed Said was persuaded to shift his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar on their recommendation.

The Khimji family achieved remarkable historical prominence when Kanaksi Khimji was conferred the title of Sheikh by the Sultan - marking the first ever use of this title for a member of the Hindu community in Oman. This unprecedented honor reflected the family's significant influence and integration into Omani society. The family's business empire, the Khimji Ramdas Group, evolved across multiple sectors including infrastructure, consumer products, and retail, establishing itself as one of the oldest trading companies in the region.

In modern times, the family has expanded their influence beyond business into sports administration and government advisory roles. The current generation continues this legacy through Pankaj Khimji, who serves as chairman of Oman Cricket (a position previously held by his father Kanak Khimji) and was appointed Foreign Trade Advisor to the Omani government by Royal Decree in January 2022. The family's influence extends internationally through cricket administration, with Pankaj Khimji serving as an Associate Member Director of the International Cricket Council and vice-president of the Asian Cricket Council, while also co-hosting the 2021 ICC Men's T20 World Cup during his chairmanship.
data.description: A prominent Gujarati merchant family in Oman, founders of the Khimji Ramdas Group established in 1870, with significant roles in business, cricket administration, and government advisory positions in modern times.
data.originStory: Part of the historic Gujarati merchant community that migrated to Oman, with their business roots tracing back to the establishment of Khimji Ramdas Group in 1870, belonging to the broader tradition of Gujarati traders who have maintained presence in Oman since the 16th century.
data.modernStatus: Directors of the Khimji Ramdas Group operating in infrastructure, consumer products, and retail sectors; current generation holds prominent positions including chairman of Oman Cricket and Foreign Trade Advisor to the Omani government

data.history:
Ahl al-Buwait is a major clan-level division within the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam according to tribal genealogists including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari. The ancestral homes of Al-Dhafeer trace back to al-Dhaghn, a place opposite Medina in the Hejaz. The designation 'Ahl al-Buwait' originated as a laqab (nickname) for the al-Suwait branch due to their habit of defending refugees, and this name eventually came to encompass one of the two main divisions of the Al-Dhafeer tribal structure.

Within the broader Al-Dhafeer tribal organization, Ahl al-Buwait represents one of two primary divisions and encompasses four distinct sub-branches: Al-Suwait, Al-Sa'id, Bani Hussein, and Al Katheer. The tribe historically inhabited the deserts of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), which corresponds to areas of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The Al-Dhafeer were known for their mobile lifestyle, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration rather than maintaining permanent settlements, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans.'

Historically, Ahl al-Buwait participated in significant regional conflicts as part of the broader Al-Dhafeer confederation. In 1782, Dhafeer fighters, including those from Ahl al-Buwait, participated in the Battle of Zubarah, helping the House of Khalifa defeat Persian forces and end Persian control over Bahrain. The tribe later faced conflict with Ikhwan forces at the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, where Al-Dhafeer successfully defended themselves in the southern Iraqi desert, killing 72 Ikhwan fighters including the notable Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish.

Around 1926, then emir Ibn Saud conquered a large portion of Arabia and allowed the Al-Dhafeer tribe's branch in Saudi Arabia, including Ahl al-Buwait, to settle in several settlements after securing roads. Most members of the tribe now live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain and Turkey.

data.history:
Al-Askar is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and other genealogical sources including Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the Al-Dhafeer tribe genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy, originating from a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina. Al-Askar appears as one of the sub-branches within the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, and Al-'Aljanat.

The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe, of which Al-Askar is part, historically lived in the desert regions of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), covering areas of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. Earlier in their history, they inhabited the desert of central Najd, and before that, the desert east of Medina. The tribe was known for their mobility, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and immigration rather than maintaining fixed settlements. They earned the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children.

Al-Askar, as part of the Al-Dhafeer confederation, participated in significant historical events. During the Persian-Bahrain conflict of 1782, when Bahrain was under Zand Persian control, the tribe contributed cavalry and fighters to help Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa and the House of Khalifa, alongside Kuwaiti forces, in the Battle of Zubarah which ended Persian control. The tribe also fought in the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, where Al-Dhafeer forces defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe in the southern desert of Iraq, killing 72 of their opponents including notable leader Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish.

Following Ibn Saud's conquest of large portions of Arabia around 1926, the Al-Dhafeer tribe, including the Al-Askar branch, was allowed to settle in several settlements after roads were secured. Today, most members of the tribe live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers, with smaller numbers residing in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey. The name 'Askar' itself relates to military or soldier origins, reflecting the tribe's historical warrior traditions and military service connections.

data.history:
The Al Bu Muhair (singular Al Muhairi, Al Mheiri, or Al Mehairi) are a tribe of the United Arab Emirates closely associated with the Bani Yas confederation of Abu Dhabi. According to the research, they are listed as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated from Najd and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700. The Al Bu Muhair became widely distributed throughout the western coastal areas of the Emirates, making them one of the most geographically dispersed tribes in the region. Their first documented migration occurred in the 1750s when they settled in Ras Al Khaimah from Abu Dhabi with the establishment of Al Qawasim rule.

By the early 19th century, the Al Bu Muhair had established significant populations across multiple Emirates under various rulers. However, political tensions arose in the 1840s when approximately 500 members became dissatisfied with the rule of Maktoum bin Butti in Dubai, who had established the emirate after leading the Al Bu Falasah migration from Abu Dhabi in 1833. This dissatisfaction led to a major migration in 1841 when these 500 Al Bu Muhair left Dubai and settled in Sharjah, followed by another contingent in 1852. The departure, combined with a plague outbreak in Dubai, contributed to the temporary abandonment of Dubai and its subsequent sacking by forces from Abu Dhabi under Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan.

By the turn of the 20th century, the Al Bu Muhair had transitioned from a largely nomadic lifestyle to become mostly settled people, with only a minority of about 20 households in Abu Dhabi maintaining a Bedouin lifestyle. Their population was distributed across the Emirates with significant concentrations: 500 in Abu Dhabi town, 500 in Al Bateen, 400 houses in Dubai, and smaller communities throughout the northern emirates including 120-200 houses in Ras Al Khaimah, 80 houses in Ajman, 60 houses each in Al Khan and Sharjah, and 30 houses in Umm Al Quwain. Historical records also document their military activities beyond the Gulf region, including a conflict with the Niquilus tribe in Oman over pearl fishing grounds in the early 1570s that resulted in the Niquilus being forced to migrate to Portuguese territory.

As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Al Bu Muhair trace their genealogical origins to Adnani Arab lineage through Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, connecting them to the same ancestral line as other prominent Gulf tribes. Their wide distribution and significant presence across multiple Emirates made them influential in the political and economic development of what would become the United Arab Emirates, with their migrations and settlements reflecting the complex tribal dynamics and political changes that shaped the region during the 18th and 19th centuries.

data.history:
The Al-Huwaitat are a large Hashemite Ashraf tribal confederation tracing their lineage to Husayn ibn Ali through their ancestor Alayan al-Jammazi al-Husayni al-Hashimi. According to their genealogical tradition, Alayan traveled from Medina to the Syrian desert with other ashraf but fell sick during his journey and was taken in by Atiyyah, chief of the Ma'azah tribe at al-Aqaba. Being the only literate person in the vicinity, Alayan supervised accounts for the Hajj pilgrimage and was called 'al-Huwayt' (the little wall) for preventing fraudulent activities. He married his benefactor's daughter, became sheikh after his father-in-law's death, and drove out the Ma'azah tribe, establishing the Huwaitat confederation.

The tribe developed into a major confederation with several branches including the Ibn Jazi, Abu Tayi, Anjaddat, and Sulaymanniyin, inhabiting areas of present-day southern Jordan, the Sinai Peninsula, parts of Egypt's Sharqia governorate, the Negev in Israel, and northwestern Saudi Arabia. During the Ottoman period, they were recorded as the only tribesmen living in the southern, inland area of the Karak Sanjak in the 16th century. They initially combined farming in the fertile areas of al-Sharat with pastoralism, but early in the 20th century became more nomadic due to rivalry between sheikhs Abtan ibn Jazi and Auda Abu Tayi, who concentrated on raiding, tribute collection, and camel-herding.

During World War I's Arab Revolt, the confederation split along political lines. The Abu Tayi section under Auda Abu Tayi supported the Hashemite cause and played a crucial role in helping Prince Feisal capture Aqaba, forming an important part of Feisal's forces. Conversely, the Ibn Jazi section remained loyal to the Ottoman Empire under their leader Hamad ibn Jazi, who was decorated by the empire in early 1917. In 1938, the Huwaitat tribe in Transjordan was estimated at around 1,000 tents, residing in the Ma'an and Aqaba region, around Tafilah, and in the plain of ash-Sher'ah in Edom.

In modern times, the Huwaitat have largely settled into villages, abandoning their nomadic lifestyle. However, they have faced severe displacement pressures from Saudi Arabia's NEOM project. In April 2020, Abdul Rahim al-Huwaiti was killed by Saudi security forces after posting videos opposing the forced eviction of tribe members from their historic homeland to make way for The Line development. The incident sparked international attention, with former Saudi intelligence officer Colonel Rabih Alenezi confirming he had received orders to use lethal force against tribal resistance. In May 2023, three tribe members - Shadli, Ibrahim, and Ataullah al-Huwaiti - received death sentences for opposing the NEOM development, while eight cousins of Abdul Rahim were arrested for protesting eviction orders.

folkLegends.title: The Little Wall
folkLegends.story: The ancestor Alayan al-Jammazi al-Husayni al-Hashimi, traveling from Medina with other ashraf, fell sick and was taken in by Atiyyah, chief of the Ma'azah tribe at al-Aqaba. As the only literate person in the area, he supervised Hajj pilgrimage accounts and prevented fraudulent activities. The local people called him 'al-Huwayt' (the little wall) because his learning was a fence against their frauds. This name was then adopted by his descendants.

Al-Ma'adin (المعادين) is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which genealogically descends from Banu Lam, a subdivision of the larger Tayy confederation. According to Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', as well as Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari's 'Masālik al-abṣār fī mamālik al-amṣār', the Al-Dhafeer genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy, originating from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn. Al-Ma'adin formed part of the Ahl al-Sumada division within the broader Al-Dhafeer tribal structure, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar.

The Al-Dhafeer confederation, including Al-Ma'adin, spread throughout Arabia through raiding activities and migration patterns, as they did not maintain fixed settlements in their early period. The tribe earned the distinctive nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of conducting raids while accompanied by women and children, as well as their notable enthusiasm for celebrating victories. Their traditional territories encompassed the desert regions of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), which included areas of present-day south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait.

A significant turning point came around 1926 when Ibn Saud conquered large portions of Arabia and established greater territorial control. Following these conquests, the Al-Dhafeer branches, including Al-Ma'adin, were permitted to establish permanent settlements after Ibn Saud secured the major roads and trade routes. This transition marked the end of their traditional nomadic lifestyle and the beginning of their integration into the emerging Saudi state structure.

In the modern era, Al-Ma'adin members, like other Al-Dhafeer branches, are primarily found in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, with smaller populations residing in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey. Contemporary tribal members have integrated into modern society, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers, while maintaining their tribal identity and genealogical connections to the broader Tayy confederation.

Al Nasur (Al Nassour) is documented as one of the authentic Arab Huwala tribes, representing original Arabs who migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Iranian coast of the Persian Gulf between the 13th and 19th centuries. The tribe was part of a larger tribal confederation known as the Huwala, which included other authentic Arab tribes such as the Qawasem, Hammadi, Obaidli, and Bani Tamim. These tribes were collectively referred to as 'Arab El-Sahel' (Arabs of the Eastern Coast) and maintained their Sunni Islamic faith, distinguishing them from the majority Twelver Shia Persian population in Iran.

According to contemporary historians including Niebuhr, Lorimer, and David Seton, the true Huwala Arabs, including Al Nasur, inhabited a specific geographical region from Bandar Kanj in the south to Bandar Kangan in the north, extending from the Arabian Gulf coast to the Shibkoh mountains in the east. This region was primarily in what is now Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, particularly around areas near Bandar Lengeh, Qishm, and Bandar Abbas. The Al Nasur, like other Huwala tribes, were involved in various maritime activities including sailing, trading, fishing, pearl diving, and cultivation along the Iranian coast.

Saudi historian Jalal Al-Haroon classifies Al Nasur as part of the first type of Huwalas - the original Arabs who migrated to Southern Iran from Coastal Eastern Arabia during the 16th and 17th centuries. The tribe experienced significant return migration to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the final major wave occurring in the 1930s due to restrictive economic policies imposed by Reza Shah Pahlavi. This return migration was part of a larger pattern affecting most Huwala tribes, who faced economic restrictions and cultural pressures under Iranian centralization policies.

In the contemporary period, Al Nasur maintains tribal identity in the Gulf states, having successfully reintegrated into Arabian Peninsula society following their return from Iran. The tribe's historical experience reflects the broader pattern of Arabian tribal mobility across the Persian Gulf, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal territories and the enduring connections between Arabian tribes and their ancestral homelands despite centuries of residence on the Iranian coast.

The research reveals three distinct historical figures and dynasties associated with the name Sa'id, but no evidence of a unified 'Al-Sa'id' tribal entity. The most prominent is the Al Bu Said dynasty of Oman, founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi in 1744. This dynasty traces its lineage through the Azd tribe, specifically to al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally migrated from Yemen following the destruction of the Marib Dam and established themselves as a leading force in early Islamic conquests under generals like Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), who became the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. Ahmad bin Said rose to power after successfully defending Sohar against Persian forces for nine months, leading to his election as Imam on November 20, 1744, marking the beginning of one of Arabia's oldest surviving royal dynasties.

Under Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' the Al Bu Said dynasty reached its zenith as a commercial and political maritime power. The empire extended from Oman to Zanzibar, parts of the East African coast including Mombasa and Pemba Island, southern Iran including Bandar-Abbas, and Gwadar in Balochistan. Said established Zanzibar as his second capital in 1832 and initiated large-scale clove cultivation. The empire was divided in 1856 after his death, creating separate sultanates in Oman and Zanzibar. The Zanzibar branch ended with the 1964 revolution, while the Omani branch continues under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq who succeeded Sultan Qaboos in 2020.

Separately, the research documents Sa'id ibn Zayd (c. 593-671), a companion of Prophet Muhammad from the Adi clan of Quraysh. He was instrumental in the conversion of Umar ibn al-Khattab to Islam and served as Muhammad's secretary, recording Quranic verses. Later he served as Governor of Kufa under Caliph Muawiya. Another historical figure was Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi (845/855-913/914), a Persian founder of the Qarmatian state in Bahrayn who established a communal society based on egalitarian principles. These represent distinct lineages and are not connected to any overarching 'Al-Sa'id' tribal confederation.

data.history: The Almuhabida is one of five primary branches of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen, the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, before undertaking significant migrations that would shape their tribal distribution across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient, tribal councils were convened to determine their future path. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes - many of whom were clans and sub-clans of the related Harb tribe - they chose migration as their solution, with those united by their fifth grandfather deciding to leave their ancestral lands together. The documented migration route took the Mahamid northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled temporarily before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region, where they established a significant presence, outnumbering the largest local tribes and forming alliances with Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. In Karak, they established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily governed by the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period until their harsh treatment of subjects prompted various clans to unite against them. In modern Saudi Arabia, the Almuhabida represents one of the five main Mahamid branches alongside alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir, and alrathea, maintaining their tribal identity within the broader Arabian tribal system while the greater Mahamid network extends throughout the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa.

Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in Karak where they established the Emirate of Mahamid

Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is one of the largest and most prominent Arabian tribal confederations, with pre-Islamic origins as part of the Rabiʿa branch of Adnanites. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib, all sharing common ancestry. Two main branches existed in early history: nomadic groups in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary populations in Al-Yamama wadis of eastern Najd, with one clan (Bani Hizzan) maintaining presence in that area to this day. During the early Islamic period, 4,000 Anizah warriors joined Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces at the Battle of Siffin, demonstrating their military significance.

By the 10th century AD, historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi recorded Anizah settlement in Hijaz, specifically Khaybar, where they expelled the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar. The 13th-century historian Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi noted that Anazah were the only Rabiʿa tribe left 'who rides horses, settles and migrates' in the Arabian Peninsula regions. During the Ottoman era, they became masters of northwestern Arabian oases, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while migrating seasonally to southern Syria where they collected khuwwa (tribute) from Hawran farmers in exchange for protection.

The major Anizah migration to Syria began with the Hasana branch in mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. Simultaneously, Wuld Ali entered Hawran, followed by Ruwallah in early 19th century. The Fad'an and Sba'a entered central Syrian plains, often conflicting with Hasana. One branch, the Ruwallah, became so powerful they practically formed an independent tribe centered around Al-Jouf and Wadi Sirhan, extending into Jordan and Syria. They became arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe, competing for the same territories.

In Najd, Anizah penetration occurred gradually, with Ibn La'bun recording settlement of several families in the 14th century. During the 15th century, Anizah migrations significantly contributed to Al-Qassim region settlement in northern Najd. In early 18th century, Bedouin Anizah reached Riyadh's gates, killing ruler Zayd Abazara'a in tribal warfare. With the First Saudi State's rise, Anizah adopted a favorable attitude but took little active military role due to geographical location. Notable 19th-century leader Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal unified Bedouin Anizah clans and died heroically at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, though Anizah won the battle.

The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted the Bedouin lifestyle, forcing most Anizah to settle and take citizenship in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, or Jordan while retaining tribal surnames like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili. Today, settled Anizah families are found across the Gulf states, with notable royal connections including Bahrain's House of Khalifa and Kuwait's House of Sabah both tracing ancestry to this vast tribe. The Al-Hathal family maintains traditional sheikhdom of the Al-Hblan branch, with their ancestor Hathal Ibn Adenan receiving the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from the Sharif of Makkah in the 17th century.

folkLegends.title: The Appointment of Hathal as Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh
folkLegends.story: According to tribal oral tradition, Hathal Ibn Adenan came to power when Makkah's Sharif was unable to collect zakat from one of the tribes near Makkah. Sheikh Hathal emerged and pledged to the Sharif to bring him the zakat of this tribe, successfully accomplishing this task. As a result, he was appointed as general sheikh of the Anizah tribe and given the seal and bundle of Sheikhdom, earning the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from Sharif Saad Ibn Zeid (1666-1705).
folkLegends.source: oral tradition recorded by Ibn Abar, notable Anezi historian

data.history:
The Azd is an ancient Arabian tribe with Qahtani origins, tracing its lineage through Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd. According to pre-Islamic Sabaic inscriptions from Sha'r Awtar's reign (210-230 CE), the Azd occupied lands west of Bisha, extending through the southwestern heights between al-Baha and Asir provinces in modern Saudi Arabia. Medieval Yemeni folklore claims they originally lived in Yemen until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE forced their migration across the Arabian Peninsula, though genetic analysis of the Azdite haplogroup J-BY74 suggests Northern Arabian or Levantine origins rather than purely Yemeni ancestry.

In the 3rd century CE, the Azd underwent a major tribal division under the four sons of Muzayqiya. Imran bin Amr led the bulk of the tribe to Oman, establishing the Azd presence in eastern Arabia and later expanding to southern Persia, becoming known as 'Azd Uman' after Islam's emergence. Jafna bin Amr migrated to Syria where he founded the Ghassanid kingdom, which would later produce Roman Emperor Philip the Arab (244-249 CE) and Byzantine Emperor Leo III the Isaurian (717-741 CE). Thalabah bin Amr settled in the Hijaz near Yathrib, becoming ancestor to the Aws and Khazraj tribes who later became the Muslim Ansar. Haritha bin Amr remained in the Tihamah region, fathering the Bariq, Khuza'a, and Aslam tribes.

The Azd 'Uman became the dominant Arab force in the eastern caliphate from 637 CE, driving conquests in Fars, Makran, and Sindh. They established major commercial networks from their bases in Bahrain and Oman, with their influence peaking under Abu Said Al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra who extended Muslim conquests eastward until his death in 702 CE. The Azd maintained strong trading relationships with India and established settlements of Persianized Arabians on the coasts of Kirman and Makran. Their political fortunes fluctuated with Umayyad governance changes, suffering under Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf but recovering under subsequent caliphs like Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik.

The Zahran branch of the Azd played particularly significant roles in Arabian history. The Al-Jadara family from Zahran settled in Mecca and were known as wall-masons, with their progenitor Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama reportedly being the first to reconstruct the Kaaba's walls after Abraham and Ishmael. They formed alliances with the Quraysh through marriage, notably when Kilab ibn Murrah married Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari, producing Qusay ibn Kilab, an ancestor of Prophet Muhammad. Malik ibn Fehm of Zahran became one of the first Arabians to settle in Oman and founded the Tanukh confederation centered in Al-Hira, with his son Jadhima becoming king of Tanukh in the second half of the 3rd century CE. Modern Azdite descendants include the Al Said dynasty of Oman, the Ghassanids, the Nasrid dynasty of Al-Andalus, and various Gulf ruling families including Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and Al Maktoum of Dubai.

folkLegends.title: The Great Migration from Yemen
folkLegends.story: According to traditional medieval Yemeni folklore, the Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula due to unfavorable living conditions.

folkLegends.title: The Four Sons Division
folkLegends.story: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches under the sons of Muzayqiya, with each son leading his people to different regions: Imran to Oman, Jafna to Syria, Thalabah to Hijaz, and Haritha remaining in Tihamah.

folkLegends.title: Amer the Wall-Mason
folkLegends.story: Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama of the Zahran branch is said to be the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael, earning him the title 'Amer the Builder' and his family the name 'Al-Jadara' (the Wall-Masons).

data.nameEtymology: The name Zahran is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'

data.history: Based on the available research, the name Bani Hammad appears in connection with two distinct historical contexts. The most prominently documented is the Hammadid dynasty, founded by Hammad ibn Buluggin in 1007-1008 CE in present-day Algeria. This Hammadid emirate established the fortified capital of Qal'at Bani Hammad and controlled much of the central Maghreb region, creating significant architectural monuments including the Great Mosque of Al Qala'a. However, this Algerian Hammadid dynasty appears to be of Berber Zirid origin rather than Arabian tribal origin. The research provides no direct genealogical connection between this North African Hammadid state and any Arabian Peninsula tribal group called Bani Hammad. In contemporary times, the name Banihammad appears as a family name among Emiratis, as evidenced by Fayez Banihammad from Khor Fakkan in the UAE. However, the research provides no information about the tribal genealogy, historical migrations, or traditional structure of any Arabian Peninsula tribe specifically called Bani Hammad. Without additional genealogical sources or tribal historical documentation, the relationship between these various uses of the Hammad name remains unclear and cannot be definitively established from the available evidence.

The Bani Sakher confederation traces its origins to Sakher bin Tha'labah bin Amro bin Alghouth bin Tayy, who likely lived in the 3rd century CE, making the tribe approximately 18 centuries old. Descended from the ancient Qahtanite Tayy tribe, they originally inhabited the Ha'il Region where they lived on the 'Mountain of Lions.' The confederation consists of three main groups: Al-Twaqa (headed by the Al-Fayez family), Al-Ka'abneh (headed by the Khreisha family), and Al-Khdeir. Beginning in the 16th century, the Bani Sakher migrated from the Hejaz to the Levant, gradually expanding their influence until they became the most powerful tribe in northwestern Arabia by the mid-18th century. During their northward expansion, they conquered territory from Tayma to Al-'Ula, coming into contact with the Bani Harb (resulting in tribal mixing), and engaged in numerous conflicts with the Anazzah and Al-Dhafeer tribes. They defeated Al-Dhafeer twice, including a decisive battle at Wadi Azraq where they killed Sheikh Sultan Ibn Suwit. The tribe initially moved to Al-Balqa but faced resistance from the Al-Adwan, prompting a temporary relocation to Palestine before returning and forming their first alliance with Al-Adwan against the Sardiyya tribe. The most infamous event in Bani Sakher history occurred in 1757 when Sheikh Qa'dan Al-Fayez led the devastating Hajj caravan raid that killed an estimated 20,000 pilgrims, including the Sultan's sister, after Ottoman authorities ceased payments for caravan protection. Under Fendi Al-Fayez in the 19th century, they controlled large parts of Jordan and Palestine, with Kitchener reporting in 1877 that Sheikh Fendi could muster 4,500 fighting men and held a monopoly in the camel trade to pilgrims. Their power was eventually broken by Ottoman forces in 1867, ending their practice of collecting protection money from settlements. The tribe played a crucial role in Jordan's founding when Mithqal Al-Fayez welcomed Emir Abdullah in 1921 and helped establish Amman as the capital. During the 1923 Adwan rebellion, the Bani Sakher supported the Hashemite monarchy, cementing their position as stalwart allies of the ruling family and maintaining significant political influence in modern Jordan.

folkLegends.title: The Mountain of Lions Origin
folkLegends.story: The Bani Sakher originally lived on top of the 'Mountain of Lions' in what is now the Ha'il Region, from where they conquered the territory spanning from Tayma to Al-'Ula

folkLegends.title: The Hospitality of Sheikh Ali Al-Khraisha
folkLegends.story: In the early 19th century, Abdullah Ibn Rashid and his brother arrived at the tents of Ali Al-Khraisha with a single camel while fleeing conflict. Though Sheikh Ali was absent, they were given hospitality. When their camel died during the night, Sheikh Ali Al-Khraisha met them the next day and insisted they take his own camel, claiming no guest should leave on foot. When Ibn Rashid came to power in the Nejd, this deed was remembered, leading to fifty years of alliance between Bani Sakher and the Al Rashid dynasty until 1920

data.history:
Banu Bakr ibn Wa'il was a major Adnani tribal confederation descended from Bakr ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit through the lineage of Adnan. Originally based in Najd and eastern Arabia, particularly around Hafar al-Batin which served as their permanent campground, they controlled vital trade routes between Hejaz and Mesopotamia. The confederation encompassed numerous powerful sub-tribes including Banu Hanifa (sedentary farmers in al-Yamama who founded Hadjr, predecessor to Riyadh), Banu Shayban (nomadic warriors who defeated the Sassanid Empire at the Battle of Dhi Qar before Islam), Banu Qays ibn Tha'labah, Banu Yashkur, and Banu 'Ijl, each maintaining distinct characteristics and territories. The tribe was instrumental in the lengthy Basus War against their Rabi'a cousins Banu Taghlib, demonstrating their military prowess in pre-Islamic Arabian warfare.

During Muhammad's era, various branches had complex relationships with the emerging Islamic state. Most notably, Banu Hanifa initially converted to Islam but then apostatized under the leadership of their self-proclaimed prophet Musaylima, leading to their defeat in the Ridda Wars around 632 CE at the battle of Aqraba. After Islamic conquest, many Bakr sections migrated northward to Upper Mesopotamia, giving their name to the Diyar Bakr region and later Diyarbakır in Turkey. They participated extensively in Muslim conquests of Iraq and Persia, with some reaching as far as the Caucasus and Anatolian Valley.

The 1257 Mongol destruction of Baghdad marked a turning point, scattering many branches southward. Most retreated to the Hauran region in southern Syria where they established new settlements and remained until modern times. Over the centuries, the confederation gradually fragmented - Banu Hanifa was eventually absorbed into the larger Anizah confederation according to Saudi royal genealogists, while other branches evolved into distinct tribal identities or merged with local populations across the Middle East. Despite this fragmentation, many families across Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Turkey continue to trace their lineage back to the various branches of Banu Bakr ibn Wa'il.

folkLegends.title: Bakr the Patriarch
folkLegends.story: According to tribal tradition, Bakr ibn Wa'il was the oldest son of Wa'il from his Bakry wife. They descended from an Arab clan that named their first-born sons Bakr in reference to their ancestor Bakr the Patriarch. From a young age, Wa'il and his brothers trained their sons to be desert warriors. Due to his fine characteristics and mastery, Wa'il put his son Bakr in charge of the clan. Bakr was described as a skilled horseman strong enough to travel long distances. As he matured, he formed a fighting force with family members and other Arabs into a nomadic federation, starting a war clan alongside Anizah and Banu Hanifa tribes.

data.nameEtymology: The name derives from Bakr ibn Wa'il, the eponymous ancestor. According to tribal tradition, 'Bakr' was a hereditary name given to first-born sons in reference to an earlier patriarch called 'Bakr the Patriarch'

migrationPath.description: Major migration after victory at Battle of Dhi Qar against Sassanids, settling along upper Euphrates and giving name to Diyar Bakr region
Participation in Islamic conquests under early Caliphs, spreading across Mesopotamia and reaching Tigris banks
Retreat from Mongol destruction of Baghdad, resettling in southern Syria where they remain today

data.history:
Banu Judham was a major Qahtani tribal confederation that claimed descent from Judham ibn Adi, tracing their lineage through Kahlan ibn Saba to the broader Qahtanite genealogy of southern Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogy, they belonged to the 'Amila-Judham group of Palestine within the Kahlan division of Qahtan. However, historian Werner Caskel suggested their genealogical relationships with claimed brother tribes Lakhm and Amila may have been politically constructed alliances rather than genuine blood ties. By the 5th-8th centuries CE, Judham had established themselves as a dominant confederation controlling strategic territories across the southern Levant and northwestern Arabia, from Tabuk northward through the Wadi Araba valley and Dead Sea regions, including the Balqa area around modern Amman, as well as Madyan, Ma'an, Adhruh, and extending south to Wadi al-Qura.

During the Byzantine period, Banu Judham served as foederati (tribal confederate troops) and nominally converted to Christianity, though the depth of this conversion remains disputed. Some sections maintained inclinations toward Judaism, and notably, the Jewish tribe of Banu Nadir in Medina traced their origins to Judham stock. The tribe initially resisted the rise of Islam, fighting alongside Byzantine forces at the Battle of Mu'ta in 629 and being specifically targeted during Muhammad's expedition to Tabuk in 630. Following their defeat at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636, Judham converted to Islam and became the largest tribal faction within Jund Filastin (the Palestine administrative district).

During the First Muslim Civil War, internal divisions emerged as the confederation split between two rival leaders: Natil ibn Qays, who supported Ali and insisted on maintaining their Qahtani genealogical origins, and Rawh ibn Zinba, who backed Mu'awiya and sought to claim Ma'add (Adnanite) descent to align with the Quda'a confederation. This rivalry reflected broader political and genealogical disputes of the period. Following Marwan I's victory at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, Judham maintained close alliance with the Umayyad dynasty until its fall in 750.

In later periods, various branches of the confederation dispersed across different regions. By the 10th century, the Banu Bayadh branch had established themselves in northern Sinai, while other sections fused with the Amila tribe in Galilee and southern Lebanon by the 11th century. Mamluk-era sources connect the Banu Sakhr tribe of al-Karak to Judham lineage. The confederation's influence also extended into Egypt through clans like the al-Ayed, whose leadership intermarried with the Circassian Abaza family during the Mamluk period, demonstrating the continued political significance of Judham-descended groups well into the medieval period.

data.history: The Banu La'm emerged as a tribal branch within the larger Tayy confederation, which itself belongs to the Qahtanite lineage of southern Arabian origin. According to traditional Arab genealogy, the Tayy tribe traces its ancestry back to the Kahlan division of Qahtan, making the Banu La'm part of the broader Qahtanite Arab grouping that originated from modern-day Yemen. The Qahtanites, also known as al-Arab al-Ariba, are considered by some Arab traditions to be the original Arabs, with their lineage tracing back through various genealogical accounts to either Joktan son of Eber or Jokshan son of Abraham and Keturah. The Kahlan branch of Qahtan, to which Tayy belongs, represents one of the two major divisions of Qahtanite tribes alongside Himyar, with Kahlan traditionally associated with the nomadic Arabs who spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula. The Tayy confederation, including the Banu La'm, was part of the great Qahtanite migrations that occurred over centuries, as these southern Arabian tribes expanded northward and eastward from their Yemeni homeland. During the pre-Islamic period, various Qahtanite tribes, including components of Tayy, participated in the major tribal migrations out of Yemen, with some groups like the Ghassanids establishing significant presence in Syria and the Levant by around 250 CE. The broader historical context shows that between the 7th and 14th centuries, Qahtanite tribes became deeply involved in the Arab conquests following the rise of Islam, migrating to newly conquered territories and often finding themselves in rivalry with the northern Adnanite tribes in what became known as the Qays-Yaman rivalry that persisted into the 19th century.

timelineEvents.description: The Ghassanids, part of the broader Qahtanite tribal movement, established the last major pre-Islamic Semitic migration northward out of Yemen, initially settling in the Hauran region and later spreading to Palestine and Jordan

data.history:
The Banu Sa'd (بنو سعد) were a prominent Arabian tribe belonging to the larger Hawazin confederation, descended from Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they traced their lineage to the Adnanite branch through Adnan, who is traditionally regarded as the patriarch of the Adnanite Arabs and considered a direct descendant of the prophet Abraham (Ibrahim) through his son Ishmael (Ismāʿīl). The Islamic prophet Muhammad is said to descend from this same Adnanite lineage through the genealogical chain: Adnan > Ma'add > Nizar > Mudar > Ilyas > Mudrikah > Khuzaimah > Kinanah > al-Nadr > Malik > Fihr > Ghalib > Lu'ay > Ka'b > Murrah > Kilab > Qusai > Abd Manaf > Hashim > Abd al-Muttalib > Abd Allah > Muhammad.

The Banu Sa'd gained particular historical significance as the foster tribe of Prophet Muhammad, with Halima al-Sa'diyya (Halima Sadia) serving as his wet nurse during his early childhood years. This relationship established deep connections between the tribe and the emerging Islamic community. The tribe was historically based in the Najd region during the pre-Islamic period and was considered among the leading noble tribes of Arabia during Muhammad's era. According to Islamic sources, they were described as great warriors who fought alongside Muhammad against enemies of Islam, having close family relations with the Prophet through the foster relationship.

As part of the broader Adnanite tribal confederation, the Banu Sa'd shared the traditional Arab genealogical claims that connected them to the Ishmaelite Arabs who inhabited Northern, Western, Eastern, and Central Arabia. The Adnanites were distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of Southern Arabia, who traced their lineage to Qahtan. This genealogical distinction played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian tribal politics and continued to influence tribal identities in the Islamic period. The tribe's descendants later dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula following the Islamic conquests, with many integrating into other tribal confederations or settling in various parts of the Gulf region during subsequent migrations.

folkLegends.title: Adnan's Prophecy of Muhammad
folkLegends.story: According to Islamic tradition, it has been said that Adnan, the patriarch of the Adnanite Arabs from whom Banu Sa'd claimed descent, foretold the coming of Muhammad and ordered his successors to follow him.

folkLegends.title: Foster Relationship with Prophet Muhammad
folkLegends.story: Halima al-Sa'diyya of the Banu Sa'd served as the wet nurse and foster mother of Prophet Muhammad during his early childhood, establishing the tribe as his foster family and creating lasting bonds between the tribe and the Prophet.
