data.history: The Banu Thu'al were a sub-branch of the Tayy tribal confederation, which belonged to the broader Qahtanite tribal grouping originating from southern Arabia. According to Arab genealogical tradition, the Tayy were part of the Kahlan division of the Qahtanite tribes, one of the major tribal groupings that traced their ancestry back through Saba to the legendary ancestor Qahtan. The Qahtanites were considered by some Arab traditions to be the original Arabs, known as al-Arab al-Ariba, with their homeland in modern-day Hadhramaut and Yemen. The Tayy confederation was one of the four main subgroups of the Kahlan division, alongside the Azd group, the Amila-Judham group, and the Hamdan-Madhhij group. As part of the broader Qahtanite migration patterns out of southern Arabia, the Tayy tribes, including the Banu Thu'al, participated in the northward movements that characterized pre-Islamic Arabian tribal dynamics. These migrations were part of larger demographic shifts that saw various Qahtanite tribes settle across different regions of the Arabian Peninsula and beyond, establishing themselves in territories far from their original Yemeni homeland. The tribal structure of the Qahtanites, including groups like the Banu Thu'al, was based on patrilineal descent and maintained through oral genealogical traditions that preserved their connection to their southern Arabian origins. During the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, these tribal identities remained crucial for political alliances, territorial claims, and social organization throughout the Arabian Peninsula.

The Dahaminah are a minor tribe from Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the United Arab Emirates, with their traditional homeland centered in the mountainous region of Wadi Qor in the Hajar Mountains. This wadi, running from the mountain village of Al Qor near Hatta through villages including Huwaylat, Rafaq and Al Nasla before crossing into Oman, formed one of the oldest caravan routes from the interior of the Gulf to the Batinah Coast. At the turn of the 19th century, the Dahaminah had a population of approximately 150 members who co-habited with members of the Manai'i tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor.

The tribe also occupied areas around Wadi Al Munay, the main tributary of Wadi Al Qor, alongside other tribal groups including the Bani Kaab, Maharzah, and Quwayd. The villages throughout these wadis were traditionally settled by the Dahaminah along with the Washahat and other tribes, taking advantage of the aflaj daudi (underground waterways) and falaj systems that distributed water for irrigation. These settlements supported a prosperous agricultural economy based on cultivation and sale of tobacco, production of dates, mangoes, oranges, limes, and other fruits, as well as cultivation of cereals.

Like many mountain communities in the region, Dahaminah settlements have experienced depopulation as younger generations migrate to the UAE's cities, though seasonal occupation continues during celebrations like Eid when family members return to the ancestral villages. The traditional villages along both Wadi Qor and Wadi Al Munay had forts or watchtowers for protection and maintenance, with archaeological evidence including Umm Al Nar era gravesites and Iron Age forts indicating the long historical occupation of these strategic mountain passes.

Al-Diqeisab (الدقيساب) is documented as one of the Riverine Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in an area called Dar al-Manasir. They are part of the broader Manasir tribal confederation, which constitutes one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan and should not be confused with the Manasir of the Persian Gulf region in the Arabian Peninsula. The Manasir people, including al-Diqeisab, are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language, similar to their neighboring tribes the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah. According to Sudanese genealogical records, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple potential lineages with varying accounts placing their ancestor Mansur either as a descendant of al-'Abbas (the uncle of Muhammad), as Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), or as connected to other tribal lineages including the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Diqeisab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. As riverine Manasir, al-Diqeisab pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop, and they are renowned for their skill in building mud houses. As of 1993, the broader Manasir population was approximately 30,000-33,000 residents. The relationship, if any, between this Sudanese al-Diqeisab clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal groups of similar name remains undocumented in available sources.

folkLegends.title: Mansur bin Qahtan Origin
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Descent
folkLegends.story: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Connection
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi Clan Origin
folkLegends.story: Other historians insist that they are the descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this opinion Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

data.history:
The Ghassanids were an Arab Christian tribal confederation that emerged from the Azd tribe of South Arabia in the 3rd century CE, following a legendary migration northward after the collapse of the Marib Dam. According to Arab genealogical tradition developed during the early Islamic period (7th-10th centuries), they descended from Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, though historian Brian Ulrich notes these genealogical links are historically tenuous. Christian J. Robin's epigraphic research suggests that between the 3rd-4th centuries CE, the Ghassanids first dominated the Hejaz region around Medina before migrating north to Syria, which became their primary base of operations. Their earliest recorded appearance was in 473 CE when their chief Amorkesos signed a treaty with the Byzantine Empire acknowledging their status as foederati controlling parts of Palestine.

Establishing their capital at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, the Ghassanids became the most powerful Arab ally of the Byzantine Empire by the 6th century, serving as a buffer against rival Arab tribes and the Persian-allied Lakhmids. Under rulers like al-Harith ibn Jabalah (r. 529-569), who received the highest imperial titles from Emperor Justinian I, they controlled territory from southern Syria to the northern Hijaz and commanded all Arab confederates of the Byzantine Empire. The Ghassanids were fervent Miaphysite Christians who played crucial roles in church politics, sponsoring the ordination of Jacob Baradaeus and other bishops while maintaining extensive monastic networks. They frequently clashed with the Lakhmid kingdom in proxy wars between the Byzantine and Sassanian empires, and participated in major campaigns during the Byzantine-Sassanian War of 602-628.

The dynasty faced periodic tensions with Byzantium due to theological disputes, including a temporary dissolution of their confederation under Emperor Maurice (582-585), though they were later restored due to military necessity. Their end came with the Islamic conquests - their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham led Byzantine forces at the decisive Battle of Yarmuk in 636, and after briefly converting to Islam, ultimately withdrew with his followers to Byzantine Anatolia in 639. Significant Ghassanid remnants remained in Damascus and the Ghouta region, many gradually converting to Islam and serving the Umayyad caliphate as governors, commanders, and scholars. Prominent families like those of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani (d. 750s) and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani (d. 833) continued as respected Islamic scholars into the 9th century, with some Ghassanids playing key roles in supporting the Umayyad restoration during tribal summits at their old capital of Jabiyah.

folkLegends.title: The Scattering of Azd
folkLegends.story: According to the 'Scattering of Azd' story, the Azd tribe migrated northward from South Arabia after the collapse of the Marib Dam, with different groups splitting off in different directions. The Ghassan were one such group that eventually settled within the Roman limes, establishing connections with other Azdi groups including the Ansar tribes of Medina.

data.nameEtymology: The name derives from their ancestor Jafna according to Arab genealogical tradition, leading to their alternate designation as 'Jafnids' by medieval Arabic authors. Ancient sources record similar tribal names: Kassanitai in Ptolemy's Geography, Casani in Pliny the Elder, Gasandoi in Diodorus Siculus, and Kasandreis in Photios I of Constantinople.

data.history: The al-Hamdatiab are one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, specifically noted in tribal records to have Shaiqi origins, distinguishing them from other Manasir sub-groups. They inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in what is collectively known as Dar al-Manasir, alongside nine other riverain clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Farei'ab, and al-'Amasib. The broader Manasir people constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan, representing an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and Arabic as their first language. Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-Hamdatiab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations, thereby uniting themselves in their claims for land and other resources against neighboring tribes. The riverain Manasir, including al-Hamdatiab, pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with their most important cash crop being a wide variety of dates sold on the national market. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses (Galus) and historically used to float wood from the Atbarah region. The total Manasir population was estimated at approximately 30,000-33,000 in the 1990s, though this figure encompasses all Manasir clans collectively rather than al-Hamdatiab specifically.

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi Clan Origin
folkLegends.story: According to one historical tradition, the Manasir including al-Hamdatiab are descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this tradition, their ancestor Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Connection
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Descent
folkLegends.story: According to current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad.

data.history: Hudhayl (بنو هذيل) was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Hudhayl ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar, making them part of the Adnani lineage through their common ancestor with the Kinana tribe. According to Ibn Ishaq, their genealogical line traces back through Mudar, Nizar, Ma'ad, and Adnan to Ishmael and Abraham. Hudhayl was the brother of Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah, whose son Kinana became the progenitor of Banu Kinana, establishing their close relationship to the ancestors of Prophet Muhammad through this shared Mudrikah lineage. The tribe inhabited the mountainous regions between Mecca and Medina in the Hijaz and became renowned throughout Arabian history for their talented poets, skilled archers, and fierce warriors who controlled strategic routes in central Arabia. During the pre-Islamic period, specific sub-groups of the tribe worshipped the god Suwa' according to Islamic sources, reflecting the polytheistic religious practices common in the region before Islam. The tribe produced notable companions of the Prophet, including Abdullah ibn Masud, as well as distinguished poets like Abū Dhuʾayb al-Hudhalī who was contemporary with Muhammad. In 930 CE, during the infamous Qarmatian Sack of Mecca, Banu Hudhayl demonstrated their martial prowess and loyalty to Islamic sacred sites when they ambushed the retreating Qarmatian forces who had desecrated the Kaaba and stolen the Black Stone. The Hudhayl warriors successfully liberated many prisoners and recovered much of the stolen loot before the Qarmatians managed to escape back to Bahrain, as part of a larger ongoing conflict between the tribe and the radical Ismaili sect. This action exemplified their role as defenders of the holy sites and their continued importance in early Islamic history. Today, the descendants of Banu Hudhayl are found primarily in Saudi Arabia, with populations also present in Tunisia, Jordan, and Egypt, maintaining their historical reputation for scholarship and cultural contributions.

The Juwarin (Al Juwarin) were a tribal group that formed part of the Al Ajwad division within the larger Al-Muntafiq tribal confederation of southern Iraq and Kuwait. As documented in historical records, the Juwarin were among the constituent tribes of this major confederation that traced its genealogy to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The Al-Muntafiq confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and later held control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. The confederation's tribal structure was complex and heterogeneous, with the leadership traditionally held by the Al-Saadun family (Sharifs from Mecca), while constituent tribes like the Juwarin had varied origins within the broader Arabian tribal framework.

During the Ottoman era, the Juwarin, along with other Muntafiq tribes, gradually transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. The broader Muntafiq confederation converted to Shia Islam from the late eighteenth century onwards, though religious composition varied among constituent tribes. Historical accounts from the early 20th century describe the Juwarin as semi-nomadic bedouins who would migrate seasonally alongside other Muntafiq tribes including the Albu Salih, Shuraifat, and Al Aunan.

These migrations followed traditional routes through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trading purposes before returning north to their tribal territories. The Juwarin would travel with well-armed tribal groups during autumn migrations to northeastern Arabia, encompassing southern Iraq and northern and western Kuwaiti territories. They would engage in trade with local villages and towns in Kuwait before returning north for the summer season. By the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of nomadic elements within the broader Muntafiq confederation, including the Juwarin, had settled in countries and regions that were part of or close to the traditional Muntafiq territories.

The Juwarin's documented alliance connections beyond the immediate Muntafiq confederation indicate their involvement in broader Gulf tribal dynamics and political relationships. Like other Muntafiq constituent tribes, many Juwarin today are likely distributed across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with some members potentially among the stateless 'bedoon' populations in Kuwait who belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation.

The Khawatir (singular Khateri الخاطري) is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates and northern Oman, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe alongside the Al Bu Shamis and Al Bu Kharaiban. Originally a Bedouin tribe, the Khawatir's traditional territory (dar) stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, with most settling around the Buraimi oasis. In 1906, Lorimer documented approximately 500 Khawatir living as nomadic Bedouin in the Jiri Plain, possessing substantial wealth including 800 camels, 70 donkeys, 1,500 sheep and goats, and 100 cattle. An additional 150 houses of settled Khawatir were recorded at Hafit southeast of Buraimi, with 900 date palms, 1,000 sheep and goats, and 200 camels.

Resentful of encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies in the 19th century, the Khawatir adopted Wahhabism as their doctrine. Following Saudi withdrawal in 1871, they submitted to Muscat but later became dependent on Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan after being pressured by his expansion in the Buraimi/Al Ain oasis area. The tribe frequently clashed with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but generally allied with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. By the 1940s, the Khawatir were considered independent of Muscat, but came under Saudi influence again in the 1950s, participating in the Buraimi dispute.

Beyond Buraimi, the Khawatir also settled in Dhaid, a Sharjah dependency, where tensions with Bani Qitab neighbors culminated in Khawatir victory in 1927. The following year, a settlement led by Na'im chiefs resulted in the ascension of deposed Sharjah Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Qasimi to rule Dhaid, with Khawatir support for his failed attempt to retake Sharjah, which was prevented by British intervention. At the turn of the 20th century, Dhaid consisted of some 140 houses owned by sections of the Tanaij, Bani Qitab and Khawatir tribes, with the town maintained for the Ruler of Sharjah by a walī who collected tithes in the form of dates and water rates.

data.history: The research provided reveals that 'Kuruni' refers to a village in Suriname, South America, inhabited by indigenous Tiriyó people of the Aramayana subgroup, also known as the Bee people. This entity has no connection whatsoever to Arabian Peninsula tribal structures or genealogy. The village has a population of 88 people as of 2022 and is located in the Coeroeni resort in the Sipaliwini District of Suriname. The area's modern history began in 1959 with the construction of the Coeroenie Airstrip for mineral exploration access. A workers' camp was established in 1965 near the airstrip for a planned weir project, though permanent habitation was not originally intended. The site became involved in the Tigri border conflict when on December 12, 1967, four armed Guyana police officers ordered workers to leave Camp Oronoque, leading to Kuruni becoming a temporary Surinamese military outpost until 1968. Permanent indigenous settlement occurred in 1995 when a small group from Kwamalasamutu moved into abandoned prefab houses, with formal leadership established in 2001-2002 when granman Asongo Alalaparu sent a Captain with his extended family to govern the village. The existing data about Kurdish origins and connections to Iranian tribal movements appears to be completely unrelated to this South American indigenous settlement.

The Mazari are a Baloch tribe of Pakistan whose name derives from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'tiger.' They are one of the oldest Baloch tribes, consisting of two primary branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. Their stronghold is Rojhan-Mazari in Rajanpur District, Punjab, near the borders of Balochistan, Sindh and Punjab. The area they conquered became known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing most of Kashmore District in Sindh, Chatha Baksha, Mazari District, and all of Tehsil Rojhan in Punjab.

Under British rule, the Mazari chief controlled territory from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The tribe engaged in significant warfare against the Sikh Empire from 1836-1838. In 1836, Mithankot was attacked by Mazari forces under Sardar Karam Khan, brother of Chief Mir Bahram Khan, in response to threats from Maharaja Kharak Singh. The Sikhs retaliated by burning Rojhan under Diwan Sawan Mal Chopra, though the Mazari evacuated in time and continued guerrilla warfare from the Sulaiman Mountains. The conflict ended in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen, meeting Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort and establishing peace.

The British colonial period saw the rise of prominent Mazari leaders including Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who became the second chief and was knighted for his services, particularly in managing tribal affairs and assisting with the western frontier. His descendants continued this legacy, with the tribe producing notable political figures in modern Pakistan including Sherbaz Khan Mazari, who served as caretaker Prime Minister. The Mazari speak Sulimanki Balochi and maintain their traditional tribal structure with the chief holding the title of Mir and Tumandar. The tribe consists of 4 large sub-clans and 60 small sub-tribes, with the Mazari Chief family belonging to the Balachani Clan.

The Mutayr (Muṭayr) is a major Arab tribe with genealogical origins traced to Ghatafan, descendants of Ishmael according to 15th-century historian Ahmad al-Qalqashandi. The tribe's original homelands were in the northern Hejaz near Medina, but in the 17th century they undertook a large-scale eastward migration into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes including Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. This migration brought them into rivalry with the Utaybah tribe, who had also recently moved into central Arabia from Hejaz. By the early 20th century, Mutayr had become the dominant nomadic tribe of the Al-Qaseem region, making them strategically important in the conflict between the Al Saud and Al Rashid clans.

Under the leadership of Faisal Al-Dewish (Al-Duwaish), Mutayr played a pivotal role in the Ikhwan movement that would shape modern Saudi Arabia. In 1912, Ibn Saud began settling nomadic tribes in agricultural villages called hijras, where they were indoctrinated into puritanical Wahhabi Islam and transformed into religious warriors known as the Ikhwan. The most important Mutayri settlement was al-Artawiyya at the northern edge of the Dahna desert. Al-Dewish enthusiastically led Mutayr's participation in the Ikhwan, providing Ibn Saud with crucial military support including the 1924 conquest of Hejaz.

Tensions arose when Ibn Saud, having completed his territorial expansion by 1925, forbade further raiding into British-protected territories of Iraq, Transjordan, and Kuwait. In late 1927, Mutayr tribesmen launched unauthorized cross-border raids, particularly targeting an Iraqi police fort at Busaiya which was part of their traditional grazing grounds. This defiance escalated into the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930), as Al-Dewish and other Ikhwan leaders accused Ibn Saud of religious laxity and abandoning true Wahhabi principles.

The revolt culminated in the Battle of Sabilla on March 29-31, 1929, where Ibn Saud's modernized forces equipped with machine guns and vehicles decisively defeated the traditional camel-mounted Ikhwan warriors, killing approximately 500 rebels. Al-Dewish fled to Kuwait in October 1929 but was detained by the British and handed over to Ibn Saud. He died in Riyadh prison on October 3, 1931, reportedly from a heart condition. The rebellion was finally suppressed on January 10, 1930, and surviving Ikhwan were incorporated into regular Saudi units, later forming the foundation of the Saudi Arabian National Guard. Today, Mutayr remains one of Saudi Arabia's largest tribes, with members settled across the kingdom and Kuwait.

The Wikipedia article on Emiratis mentions various tribal groups but does not reference the Obaidli. The Arabic Wikipedia article on Qatari architecture discusses general tribal patterns and building traditions but makes no mention of the Obaidli tribe. Rabīʿa ibn Nizār represents one of the two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes alongside Mudhar, tracing descent from Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. According to classical Arab genealogists, the confederation encompassed major tribal branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Bakr ibn Wa'il (with sub-tribes Banu Hanifa, Banu Shayban, Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba), Taghlib, and al-Nammir ibn Qasit. Like other Adnanite Arabs, legendary accounts place their original homelands in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which they migrated northwards and eastwards across the Arabian Peninsula and Mesopotamia.

Geographically, Abd al-Qays settled in Eastern Arabia including modern Bahrain and were largely sedentary, while Bakr's territories stretched from al-Yamama (around modern Riyadh) to northwestern Mesopotamia. Taghlib inhabited the eastern banks of the Euphrates, and Anizah divided between sedentary sections in southern Yamama and nomadic groups further north. Many Rabīʿa tribes, particularly Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, al-Nammir, and sections of Bakr, were predominantly Christian before Islam, with Taghlib maintaining Christian identity for some time after the Muslim conquests. Anazah and Bakr worshiped an idol called al-Sa'eer in pre-Islamic times.

During the 7th century, Mu'awiya settled Rabīʿa tribes in Diyar Rabi'a, the easternmost province of Jazira (Upper Mesopotamia), centered around Mosul and encompassing the upper Khabur River region and both shores of the Tigris from Jazirat ibn Umar to Tikrit. The confederation participated in early Islamic conquests and played significant roles during the Rashidun and Umayyad periods. The region of Diyar Bakr and later the city of Diyarbakır in southern Turkey take their names from Banu Bakr, a major Rabīʿa sub-tribe.

During the Abbasid era, members of Bani Hanifa and related Bakr tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, dominating gold mines near Aswan and intermarrying with local Beja peoples, giving rise to tribes like the Banu Kanz. The region came under various dynastic controls including the native Hamdanid dynasty in the 10th century, followed by Buyids and Uqaylids until the Seljuq conquest. Modern descendants of Rabīʿa tribes continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, with several royal families including Al Saud, Al Khalifa, and Al Sabah tracing ancestry to various branches of the confederation, though some lineage claims are disputed among genealogists.

The Shaiqiyah are an Arabized Nubian tribe primarily based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region and Dar al-Shayqiya stretching from Korti to the fourth Nile cataract. While they claim descent from Shaiq, allegedly a descendant of Abbas (the Prophet Muhammad's uncle) who came from the Arabian Peninsula in the 7th century, scholars classify them as Arabized Nubians who emerged as a distinct entity in the 15th century as a hybrid of various tribes settled in the area. By the early 20th century, tribal nobles themselves denied Arab origins, stating they were indigenous to Sudan. The tribe was historically bilingual in Arabic and Dongolawi (a Nubian language) until the late 19th century, with complete Arabization occurring by the early 20th century. The Shaiqiyah emerged as an independent political entity after the Makurian civil war of 1365, initially as subjects of the Funj Sultanate before breaking free around 1690 by defeating the Abdelab governor. They became renowned as fierce horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes along the Nile and conducted raids as far as Wadi Halfa in the north and Shendi in the south. Their military prowess was documented by early 19th century travelers like James Bruce and John Lewis Burckhardt, who described them as perfectly independent people fighting on horseback in coats of mail with lances, targets, and sabres. The tribe's independence ended in 1820 when they were defeated by the Turco-Egyptian forces at the battle of Korti under Muhammad Ali's conquest, despite initial resistance led by Mihera Bint Abboud. Following their defeat, they were incorporated into Turkish ranks and used during the suppression of the Ja'alin revolt in 1822, receiving Ja'alin lands between Shendi and Khartoum as reward for their service. During the Mahdist period (1880s-1898), the tribe suffered severe persecution, with many leaders executed, reducing them to a few hundred families by the reconquest. They later recovered and served prominently in the Egyptian Army and Sudan Defence Force, with General Ibrahim Abboud becoming Sudan's president in 1964.

The Taulantii were an ancient Illyrian people who inhabited the southeastern Adriatic coast of what is now Albania, representing one of the oldest and most significant tribal groups in classical antiquity. Their name, meaning 'swallow-men' (connected to Albanian 'dallëndyshe'), reflects totemic practices common among Illyrian peoples. First recorded by Hecataeus of Miletus in the 6th century BC, the Taulantii dominated the coastal plains between the rivers Drin and Vjosa, with their territory centered around present-day Tirana and extending between the Mat and Shkumbin valleys. Their strategic location made them crucial players in Mediterranean politics, particularly around the Greek colony of Epidamnos-Dyrrhachion (modern Durrës). The tribe included several sub-groups, notably the Chelidonioi (another name for the Taulantii themselves, translated from Illyrian by Greek colonists) and the Abroi, a northern subgroup known for their technique of preparing mead from honey.

The Taulantii reached their political and military apex under King Glaukias (ruled c. 335-295 BC), who defied Alexander the Great during the siege of Pelium in 335 BC and maintained independence when other Illyrian tribes fell under Macedonian control. Glaukias offered asylum to the infant Pyrrhus of Epirus after his father Aeacides was expelled, raising him for ten years as an adoptive son alongside his wife Beroea, who was a member of the Aeacid dynasty. In 306 BC, Glaukias established Pyrrhus on the Epirote throne with Illyrian military support, demonstrating the tribe's significant political influence beyond their borders. This relationship strengthened through marriage alliances, as Pyrrhus later married the Illyrian princess Bircenna, daughter of Bardylis II.

After Glaukias, the Taulantian territory was ruled by successive kings who continued to exercise authority over the crucial port city of Dyrrhachion. Monunius (from about 280 BC) and his successor Mytilos minted silver and bronze coins respectively in Dyrrhachion, bearing their names and the city's symbol, indicating continued control over this vital commercial center. The tribe played a significant role in the civil war at Epidamnos in 435 BC, when exiled oligarchs allied with the Taulantii to besiege the city, ultimately contributing to the broader conflict that became a pretext for the Peloponnesian War as recorded by Thucydides.

The Taulantii's power gradually declined following Roman expansion into the region. After the Roman victory in the Third Illyrian War in 167 BC, they were among the tribes rewarded with freedom by the Roman Senate for abandoning King Gentius before his defeat. However, their independence was finally ended during Octavian's Illyrian campaign of 35-33 BC, when they were defeated alongside other Illyrian peoples including the Oxyaei, Pertheenatae, Bathiatae, and others. This marked the conclusion of nearly six centuries of Taulantian prominence in Balkan politics and their integration into the Roman provincial system.

folkLegends.title: Sons of Illyrius
folkLegends.story: According to a mythological tradition reported by Appian (2nd century AD), the Taulantii were among the South-Illyrian tribes that took their names from the first generation of descendants of Illyrius, the eponymous ancestor of all Illyrian peoples. Taulas, one of the six sons of Illyrius, was considered the eponymous ancestor of the Taulantii.

data.nameEtymology: The term 'Taulantii' is connected with the Albanian word 'dallëndyshe', meaning 'swallow'. The ethnonym Chelidonioi, also reported by Hecataeus as neighboring the Taulantii, is the Greek translation of Taulantii, as 'khelīdṓn' means 'swallow' in Ancient Greek. The name suggests the practice of animal totemism, which was widespread among Illyrian peoples.

timelineEvents.description: Taulantii allied with Corinth and Corcyra in war against Liburni, leading to Greek colonization of Epidamnos-Dyrrhachion

timelineEvents.title: Epidamnos Civil War
timelineEvents.description: Taulantii allied with exiled oligarchs to besiege Epidamnos, contributing to conflict that became pretext for Peloponnesian War

timelineEvents.title: Defiance of Alexander the Great
timelineEvents.description: King Glaukias allied with Cleitus in revolt against Alexander at siege of Pelium, maintaining Taulantii independence

timelineEvents.title: Installation of Pyrrhus
timelineEvents.description: Glaukias established Pyrrhus of Epirus on throne with Illyrian military support, demonstrating Taulantii political influence

timelineEvents.title: Roman Freedom Grant
timelineEvents.description: Roman Senate granted freedom to Taulantii for abandoning King Gentius before his defeat in Third Illyrian War

timelineEvents.title: Roman Conquest
timelineEvents.description: Octavian defeated Taulantii during Illyrian campaign, ending their independence after nearly six centuries

data.history:
Based on available research, al-Wahabab (الوهاب) are documented as one of the riverine Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) along the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in Northern Sudan. The Sudanese Manasir, including the al-Wahabab clan, constitute a Sunni Arab riverine tribe of Northern Sudan who inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in an area they call Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighboring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are described as an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language.

The Manasir community, including al-Wahabab, is understood by critical research as having formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in adjoining areas. Earlier travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and strong similarities of their burial customs with that of the Nubians can still be observed.

The al-Wahabab clan is classified among the Manasir al-Nil (riverine Manasir) rather than the Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir). The riverine Manasir pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on the national market. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and historically floated wood from the region of Atbarah.

The relationship between this Sudanese al-Wahabab clan and any Arabian Peninsula family remains unclear from available sources. The existing data's association with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and the Saudi religious establishment is not supported by the research provided, which focuses exclusively on the Sudanese Manasir context.

folkLegends.title: Mansur bin Qahtan Origin
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Descent
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi al-Hankab Migration
folkLegends.story: Other historians insist that they are descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this opinion Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Lineage
folkLegends.story: According to current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad.

data.history:
Banu Uqayl was a major Arab tribe belonging to the Banu Ka'b branch of the large Banu Amir confederation, which had its original homeland in the western Arabian Peninsula on the border between the Hejaz and the Najd. The tribe traced its Adnani lineage through the genealogy: Uqayl ibn Ka'b ibn Rabi'ah ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah ibn Mu'awiyah ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin. The Uqayl branch moved southwards from their original territory and settled in the large valley known as al-Aqiq (modern-day Wadi al-Dawasir), which they later claimed was granted to them by the Prophet Muhammad. During the Abbasid Caliphate, most of the Banu Amir moved from the Najd into Iraq and Syria, with the Uqaylids being among the last to leave.

Settling on the banks of the Euphrates, a section of Banu Uqayl founded the Uqaylid dynasty in the 10th century, which controlled Mosul and other regions of Upper Mesopotamia while remaining largely nomadic. When the Uqaylid dynasty fell, three large Uqaylid tribes - the Khafaja, the Ubadah, and al-Muntafiq - settled in Lower Mesopotamia and remain there to the present day. Another section of the Uqayl, possibly coming from Iraq according to Ibn Khaldun, took over the deserts of eastern Arabia around al-Ahsa Oasis, where they allied with the Qarmatians like many other Banu Amir groups.

After the Qarmatians fell to the Uyunid dynasty in 1076, Usfur ibn Rashid deposed the Uyunids in the mid-13th century and founded the Usfurid dynasty, which lasted until 1330. The most powerful Uqaylid dynasty were the Jabrids, who rose in the early 15th century when Ajwad ibn Zamil's elder brother deposed the last Jarwanid ruler in Qatif. At their height, the Jabrids controlled the entire Arabian coast on the Persian Gulf, including the islands of Bahrain, and regularly led expeditions into central Arabia. One contemporary scholar described Ajwad ibn Zamil as 'the king of al-Ahsa and Qatif and the leader of the people of Najd.' The Jabrids lost Bahrain to the Portuguese in 1521 and their kingdom collapsed soon afterwards, though one branch remained active in Oman for nearly three centuries.

The fate of the non-Omani Jabrids remains uncertain. Some historians believe they are identical with the 'Jubur' section of the Bani Khalid, who eventually took control of the region after the Jabrids. The Bani Khalid tribe itself is believed to be partly of Uqaylid origin. Notable members of Banu Uqayl throughout history included Ibrahim ibn Asim al-'Uqayli (Umayyad commander and governor of Sistan), Ishaq ibn Muslim al-Uqayli (Umayyad commander and governor of Arminiya), Layla al-Akhyaliyya (Umayyad classical poet), Muhammad ibn al-Musayyab (chieftain and founder of Uqaylid Emirate), and Ibn Mulhim (Fatimid commander and governor of Aleppo).

folkLegends.title: Grant of al-Aqiq Valley
folkLegends.story: The Uqayl branch claimed that the large valley known as al-Aqiq (modern day Wadi al-Dawasir) was granted to them by the Prophet Muhammad when they settled there after moving southwards from their original homeland.

timelineEvents.title: Foundation of Uqaylid Dynasty
timelineEvents.description: Uqaylid section founded dynasty in Mosul and Upper Mesopotamia

timelineEvents.title: Fall of Qarmatians to Uyunids
timelineEvents.description: Qarmatians fell to the Uyunid dynasty in eastern Arabia

timelineEvents.title: Usfur ibn Rashid founds Usfurids
timelineEvents.description: Usfur ibn Rashid deposed the Uyunids and founded the Usfurid dynasty

timelineEvents.title: Fall of Usfurids
timelineEvents.description: Usfurid dynasty ended, area taken over by Jarwanid dynasty

timelineEvents.title: Rise of Jabrids
timelineEvents.description: Ajwad ibn Zamil's elder brother deposed last Jarwanid ruler and founded Jabrid dynasty

timelineEvents.title: Death of Ajwad ibn Zamil
timelineEvents.description: Death of most prominent Jabrid ruler who controlled entire Arabian Gulf coast

timelineEvents.title: Portuguese Conquest of Bahrain
timelineEvents.description: Jabrids lost Bahrain to Portuguese, kingdom collapsed soon afterwards
